annotations.text: One syndrome that deserves mention in this case is arrhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia, which is primarily a right ventricular cardiomyopathy, frequently with little or no left ventricular involvement.
ustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia is often associated with a poor outcome in patients with heart disease.
The presence of sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia is often associated with a poor outcome in patients with heart disease.
Although this patient seems to have had disproportionate right ventricular involvement, other clinical findings would not be explained by this diagnosis.
The pattern of lung involvement, although atypical, is consistent with pulmonary sarcoidosis.
the cardiomyopathy, conduction-system dysfunction, and sustained ventricular tachycardia are common manifestations of cardiac sarcoidosis.
Generalized cutaneous anergy is typical of sarcoidosis.
The diagnosis of sarcoidosis should be strongly considered in any young patient with conduction-system disease and ventricular tachycardia.
I believe that this patient had systemic sarcoidosis involving the heart and lungs.
I have seen both conduction-system disease and ventricular tachycardia as complications of giant-cell myocarditis.
The patient under discussion had apparently been exposed to tuberculosis and had been told of several positive tuberculin tests.
Myocardial involvement, which is rare, occurs most often in patients with miliary tuberculosis, and its characteristic features were absent in this patient.
Generalized anergy is more consistent with lymphoma or sarcoidosis.
The diagnosis of sarcoidosis should be strongly considered in any young patient with conduction-system disease and ventricular tachycardia.
The predominant right-upper-lobe reticulonodular infiltrate and lymphadenopathy in this patient were consistent with pulmonary sarcoidosis.
Although tuberculous myocarditis is a possibility in this case, several findings provide evidence against this diagnosis.
I believe that this patient had systemic sarcoidosis involving the heart and lungs.
Granulomatous involvement of the heart can cause either a restrictive or a dilated cardiomyopathy.
Frequent clinical manifestations include conduction abnormalities and ventricular tachyarrhythmias.

According to one report, 4 of 52 patients presented with unilateral findings on films of the chest: 1 with right hilar lymphadenopathy and 3 with right-lung infiltrates; in 1 of these patients the process ultimately became bilateral.

The presentation may also include myocarditis with congestive heart failure, sudden cardiac death, supraventricular tachyarrhythmia, mitral-valve insufficiency due to papillary-muscle dysfunction, formation of a left ventricular aneurysm, acute myocardial infarction, and pericarditis.
Congestive heart failure can be seen in either acute or chronic disease.
The patient under discussion had apparently been exposed to tuberculosis and had been told of several positive tuberculin tests.
Upper-lobe involvement simulating pulmonary tuberculosis was found in 54 of 616 patients (9 percent) at one institution and was often unilateral.
Although 20 to 25 percent of patients with sarcoidosis have cardiac involvement at autopsy, only 5 percent of them have clinically overt disease.
A substantial number of patients with cardiac sarcoidosis do not have signs of extracardiac involvement.

Percutaneous endomyocardial biopsy is diagnostic in less than 30 percent of patients with cardiac sarcoidosis, probably because it is difficult to perform a percutaneous biopsy of the base of the septum and left ventricular free wall, and the lesions have a patchy distribution.
The relative frequency of cardiac involvement in sarcoidosis varies according to race.
According to Sharma, myocardial sarcoidosis is difficult to diagnose, has a treacherous course that may be fatal, and responds poorly and randomly to therapy.

Cor pulmonale may develop because of pulmonary fibrosis or selective vascular obliteration by granulomas, but it is relatively uncommon and has not developed in this patient despite radiologic evidence of extensive disease.
The case under discussion is unusual in that biopsies of both organs were performed.
The diagnostic procedure was probably a transbronchial biopsy at the time of bronchoscopic examination.
